summary:
Decisional algorithms like the Naranho scale provide limited, probabilistic estimates of ADR causality and lack the ability to capture essential contextual factors—such as timing, comorbidities, and biologic plausibility—that define true global introspection. Human clinicians' integrated interpretation of complex clinical narratives remains indispensable for reliable individual causality judgments in spontaneous reporting scenarios. 

The summary should be concise yet comprehensive enough to stand independently — it must contain all critical information needed to understand why one agrees/disagrees with the original claim based solely upon this argument’s logic chain.

Only include content directly related to the core issue raised—the role of algorithmic versus human-driven causality determination—in spontaneity-reported adverse events within real-world practice settings.

Avoid tangential details about other types of medical decisions or non-clinical applications unless explicitly tied back to ADR causality assessment under natural conditions where full data access may be absent.

Ensure clarity through plain language while preserving technical accuracy regarding how these methods function differently at both systemic level (algorithm design) vs personal-level cognition (clinical insight).

No markdown formatting required.

We agree with the original conclusion because decisional algorithms only produce probabilistic risk ratings lacking sufficient nuance to assess individual ADR causality accurately. They fail to integrate crucial aspects of clinical reality—including timing relationships between exposure and symptoms, patient-specific variables, and overall health trajectory—that rely entirely on human global introspection during case review. These limitations mean automated approaches cannot match—or even approximate—the depth of analytical thinking involved in evaluating spontaneous reports meaningfully across diverse cases.
